data.title: After five years of fruitless efforts, has Neynar's acquisition of Farcaster SocialFi failed?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On January 21, 2026, Neynar announced the acquisition of the decentralized social protocol Farcaster, with the founding team shifting towards wallet applications. Farcaster founder Dan Romero stated that the protocol contract, code repository, official application, and AI token issuance platform Clanker will be transferred to Neynar in the coming weeks for subsequent operation and maintenance.

Why is Farcaster transforming? Why did it choose Neynar for takeover? Is the SocialFi track really dead?

I. The Transformation Beginning Last Year: Coming for Tools, Staying for the Network

Farcaster had already given the reasons for its planned transformation last December: the social track was not ideal, and wallets were the key.

On December 8, 2025, Farcaster co-founder Dan Romero announced that the platform would undergo a major strategic adjustment, abandoning the path of finding product-market fit with a "social-first" approach over the past four years, and shifting to a growth model centered on wallets.

The team stated that the rapidly growing in-app wallet functionality is currently the module closest to product-market fit, and they will adopt a "first use the tool (wallet), then build the network (protocol)" approach to drive user conversion on the wallet side and turn them into active protocol users. Romero emphasized that the Farcaster protocol remains open and decentralized, but the official application will focus on expanding its capabilities in the cross-domain of wallet and social networking. Furthermore, Farcaster has previously acquired the token issuance platform Clanker to strengthen its related product portfolio. The full text of the post is as follows: Farcaster Status Quo, December 2025 (This week I will be writing a series of podcasts on this topic.) Varun and I have been working on Farcaster for over five years. Our goal has always been to build a scalable decentralized social network protocol with 1 billion users daily. This is a challenge, and while we have made some progress, we still have a long way to go to reach our goal. From the outset, we firmly believed in two crucial things: full decentralization and product-driven protocol development. This led us to make pragmatic, rather than dogmatic, decisions regarding the underlying protocol and to iterate extensively on our application. We never aimed to please the ultimate proponents of decentralization. For the first 4.5 years, we pursued a social-first strategy. We released a sufficiently decentralized, usable version of the protocol, allowing multiple independent teams to build and integrate on top of it without permission. However, despite numerous attempts (and some brief growth spurts), we consistently failed to find a sustainable growth mechanism similar to Twitter's social network—meaning the product and market were not yet fully aligned. Earlier this year, we launched a wallet feature within the application. The wallet has grown rapidly, and we believe this is the closest we've come to product-market alignment in five years. But how does a wallet help a social network grow? First, every user who registers, deposits, and starts using the wallet also joins the protocol. The more useful the wallet, the more users will use the protocol. Second, since a social network already exists, it's easier to build social features for the wallet that leverage the protocol. Adding a wallet to a social network is far easier than adding a social network to a wallet. Finally, we believe the best way to increase the number of protocol users is with a "come for the tool, stay for the network" strategy. (The wallet is the tool, the protocol is the network.) Does this mean the protocol has now become a casino? Not at all. The protocol is a collection of various features, such as following, interaction, identity, and wallet. As a developer, you can choose the most useful features according to your needs. The system is open. Our app focuses more on the integration of wallet and social features, but other clients, such as Uno, Recaster, Cura, Base, Zapper, and Firefly, take different approaches. As a user, you can control your experience by choosing a client. If you still want to use the Farcaster app, you can customize your experience using the following feed and keyword blocking features (e.g., try blocking "$"). Following this, in response to some user questions, Farcaster reiterated the reasons for the transformation: If you missed the previous message, let me reiterate: We are focused on building a great wallet to attract more users. Every new or retained user is a new user of our protocol. We tried a social-first strategy for four and a half years, but the results were not ideal. Wallet users have been growing, so we decided to double down on this direction. This means that new features and product positioning will be centered around the wallet. If you don't like this direction: 1. Use other clients; there are many options on the market. 2. Develop your own client. 3. Consider using other social networks.

II. Why Neynar? How will personnel be adjusted?

1. Why Neynar?

First, because Neynar is the software platform for the Farcaster ecosystem. Most Farcaster products run on Neynar infrastructure.

Neynar's long-standing positioning within the Farcaster ecosystem has been very clear: it doesn't focus on a strong consumer-facing narrative, but rather provides developers with underlying capabilities such as Farcaster API, indexing, identity resolution, data synchronization, anti-spam and growth tools. Farcaster is the protocol layer, while Neynar is the infrastructure layer that makes everything "run and scale."

"A True Account of the Collapse of Decentralized Social Networking: From High Hopes to a Disappointing Exit"In fact, most projects in the SocialFi sector suffer from the following problems:First, decentralized social projects often rely on the spontaneous migration of developers and users, but ordinary users are already accustomed to the Web2 centralized social model, and very few can truly migrate and become loyal users. Perhaps young people who are just starting to use social platforms are the user group that should be the focus of exploration.Second, compared with the frequent viral topics of centralized social platforms, Web3 decentralized social platforms attract more technical users than ordinary users. Once the content output is insufficient and not eye-catching enough, it is difficult to retain users. Finally, most decentralized social projects initially attracted users through airdrops and points systems. Once the incentive mechanism becomes unsustainable, the user surge driven by speculation inevitably fades. No matter how much hype and promotion these projects generate initially, they ultimately struggle to solve the problem of user churn. Conclusion In summary, judging from the current state of SocialFi projects, the decentralized social sector has indeed experienced a downturn. However, this may only be a temporary setback. If a truly practical application can be developed that provides users with genuine content value and combines earning and transfer functions, it might ignite a genuine decentralized social boom in the Web3 space. This is precisely the core logic behind Farcaster's shift to the wallet sector—leveraging high-frequency wallet tools to drive user growth, and then using user retention to support the social protocol ecosystem. Decentralized social is not a false proposition, but merely focusing on "social priority" and using Web2 thinking to envision the future of Web3 projects may be the crux of the problem.

data.title: Predicting the future: Ten key signs of change in the crypto world in 2026.
data.description:
2026 is gradually becoming one of the most pivotal years in the history of the cryptocurrency market. After years of innovation, regulatory changes, and shifting investor sentiment, the digital asset ecosystem is entering what many analysts call a completely new cycle. Unlike past cycles driven almost entirely by price speculation, the next phase appears to be rooted in deeper structural developments: Institutional adoption; Regulatory clarity; Real-world blockchain integration; Mature financial infrastructure. Below are the top ten cryptocurrency predictions for 2026, revealing where the market might be heading. This article discusses why this cycle might be different from anything you've seen before. Bitcoin may break the old cycle and reach a new all-time high. Experts and research reports suggest that Bitcoin could surpass its previous all-time high set in 2025, reaching new valuation levels in 2026. While predictions vary widely, some analysts believe that structural demand, including institutional interest, upcoming ETF inflows, and scarcity, could drive Bitcoin to break past peaks and reach new highs. This prospect contrasts with the view that Bitcoin strictly adheres to the traditional four-year halving cycle. Instead, the current narrative focuses more on fundamental demand drivers that could underpin long-term growth beyond simple cyclicality. The potential for renewed price strength is rooted in macroeconomic factors and the growing participation of a diversified investor base.

Institutional investment is expanding and diversifying

Institutional adoption is a defining theme in the crypto space in 2026, with research showing that market interest is shifting from speculation to strategic allocation.

Professional investors, hedge funds, and traditional asset management firms are increasingly viewing digital assets as part of their diversified portfolios. Based on analysis of institutional allocation trends, many large investors now view crypto assets as a **continuous allocation** (typically 1-2% of a multi-asset portfolio), rather than a marginal asset class. This participation is expected to expand further, from Bitcoin to a basket of products and diversified crypto strategies balancing risk-reward characteristics. Institutional flows, ETFs, and regulated investment vehicles are helping to bridge the gap between traditional finance and the crypto market. Clearer regulation will drive wider adoption. One of the biggest catalysts for crypto growth in 2026 may be regulatory clarity. Global jurisdictions are working to establish frameworks for managing digital assets, taxation, and market infrastructure. In countries like India, the upcoming 2026 regulatory bill is expected to address crypto taxation and provide guidelines aimed at boosting investor confidence. A clearer legal framework reduces uncertainty and risk, making it easier for institutional capital and everyday investors to participate. As rules regarding trading, reporting, and stablecoin issuers become clearer, market legitimacy and accessibility are likely to improve, accelerating mainstream adoption. Stablecoins are evolving into everyday financial instruments. Cryptocurrencies pegged to fiat currencies—stablecoins—are poised for significant progress in 2026. The use cases for stablecoins extend far beyond trading and speculation; they are increasingly seen as practical tools for payments, settlements, and cross-border value transfers. The United States has further legalized these assets through stablecoin-oriented legislation such as the GENIUS Act, allowing regulated institutions to issue stablecoins backed by high-quality collateral. This evolution could make stablecoins an integral part of digital payment systems, connecting fiat currencies and blockchain environments. They are expected to see wider adoption in retail commerce, corporate settlements, and even savings mechanisms this year. Real-world assets are gaining attention. Real-world assets (RWAs) are projected to be one of the most transformative trends of 2026. RWAs convert tangible financial instruments, such as bonds, real estate, or stocks, into digital tokens on the blockchain, enabling fragmented ownership, instant settlement, and greater liquidity. Large financial institutions are exploring blockchain-based trading platforms to support tokenized securities, and regulatory initiatives in this direction are underway. RWA can unlock significant capital by making traditionally illiquid assets more accessible to a wider range of investors. This, in turn, could drive demand for blockchain infrastructure in financial markets and reshape how assets are traded globally. Ethereum is consolidating its core role not only as a smart contract platform but also as a central hub for DeFi, RWA, and institutional use cases. Increased on-chain activity, shrinking liquidity supply, and rising staking participation all suggest that Ethereum may perform strongly in the coming year.

Staking ETH

(Source: Beaconcha.in)

Many analysts believe that in 2026, these factors, coupled with Ethereum's extensive developer ecosystem, may support its deeper integration with traditional finance and institutional capital. Ethereum's growing use in decentralized lending, borrowing, and tokenized asset markets could make it one of the most widely used blockchains by retail and professional investors. Prediction Markets and New Interfaces Expand Liquidity New forms of decentralized interfaces, such as prediction market platforms, are expected to gain momentum in 2026. These platforms allow users to trade contracts based on real-world events and outcomes, creating new ways to capture market sentiment and facilitate liquidity beyond traditional spot and derivatives markets. Research suggests that changing tax considerations and market preferences may help prediction markets attract broader trading activity. As these markets mature, they have the potential to become a major component of the crypto infrastructure, linking price discovery mechanisms to real economic incentives and expanding the ways investors interact with digital assets.

（Source: Coinbase 2026 Market Outlook）

Crypto Markets Remain Volatile but More Selective

data.title: Bitcoin can't wait 20 years; the quantum threat is already looming.
data.description:
Author: Youssef El Maddarsi Source: cointelegraph Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Bitcoin's 20-year quantum security window has collapsed. 25% of the Bitcoin supply is stored in high-risk addresses and urgently needs to be migrated.

Some Bitcoin supporters believe that the network will not face a substantial quantum threat in the near future—they cite the post-quantum standard approved by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), claiming that Bitcoin can be upgraded before the advent of quantum computers capable of breaking cryptography. This confidence is based on a high-risk assumption: the quantum threat will only truly arrive when quantum computers can crack keys in real time. Adam Back once suggested that Bitcoin had at least 20 to 40 years to prepare, but in fact, the quantum threat already exists.

Bitcoin cannot rely on a long upgrade path of decades.

Some readers may strongly object, insisting that the timeline for quantum technology development remains highly uncertain, and there's no need to rush into action; excessive warnings could even trigger unnecessary panic. However, the facts do not warrant our complacency. IBM recently achieved a major breakthrough in practical quantum computing. Its next-generation chips and faster error correction technology are expected to enable the company to achieve quantum supremacy by 2026 and launch an early fault-tolerant quantum system by 2029. This quantum technology race is intensifying. At the 2025 Devconnect conference, Vitalik Buterin stated that quantum computers may break elliptic curve cryptography sooner than expected, possibly even before the 2028 US presidential election, and advocated that Ethereum should transition to quantum-resistant cryptography within a few years. This contrasts sharply with the "security narrative" promoted by some Bitcoin enthusiasts, indicating that even the founder of Ethereum believes the window of opportunity for quantum threats is far more pressing than people imagine. I. Quantum Risks Already Have Market Impact A recent Deloitte report shows that approximately 4 million Bitcoins (25% of the total available supply) are stored in addresses whose public keys are vulnerable to quantum attacks. Researchers have long warned that sufficiently advanced quantum computers can use the Scholl algorithm to deduce private keys from public keys, allowing attackers to instantly empty traditional wallets. This is not a problem unique to Bitcoin. Ethereum and most current blockchains rely on elliptic curve cryptography, a foundation that quantum technology will fundamentally disrupt. Vitalik Buterin has developed contingency plans for scenarios where quantum computers could crack Ethereum accounts.

Second, the argument of "upgrading later" is not valid in reality

The core of the view that "Bitcoin has decades to prepare for quantum threats" is the belief that NIST's post-quantum cryptography (PQC) standard can be directly adopted before a substantial attack occurs. However, upgrading Bitcoin is not a simple patch fix, but a fundamental reconstruction of the protocol signature mechanism. Researchers at the University of Kent pointed out that upgrading Bitcoin to a quantum-resistant cryptographic system could require up to 75 days of downtime; if the network needs to reduce its computing power during the migration to reduce the attack surface, the downtime could even exceed 300 days. For an asset class worth trillions of dollars, a long-term global downtime is far from an acceptable "timely fix" solution for the industry.

Even if Bitcoin can achieve a smooth migration at the technical level, political realities will pose another obstacle. Bitcoin's governance culture has always resisted change, as evidenced by the fact that even the relatively mild Taproot upgrade underwent several years of debate and coordination.

The high-risk operation of forcibly migrating the entire network to a completely new encryption foundation will inevitably trigger ideological conflicts, potential chain forks, and long-term uncertainty. The idea that such a radical reconstruction can be easily implemented decades later ignores the adversarial game that Bitcoin faces in simpler upgrades. Meanwhile, the development of quantum technology is far exceeding most people's expectations. The European Commission and member states recently released a coordinated roadmap to transition the EU's digital infrastructure to post-quantum encryption, addressing the threat posed by quantum computers to existing encryption systems. The plan sets a unified timetable: all member states must launch national post-quantum encryption strategies and initial migration work by 2026; critical infrastructure and other high-risk areas must adopt quantum-resistant encryption by 2030; and by 2035, the post-quantum encryption transition of all upgradable systems must be completed. III. The Market Impact of Delayed Transition Could Be Catastrophic For the encryption industry, the urgency of this threat is also reflected in the potential market shock caused by an improper transition. If attackers use quantum hardware to crack private keys from dormant Bitcoin wallets, they could suddenly transfer millions of long-dormant Bitcoins, dumping them on exchanges and causing a price crash. Similarly, if malicious quantum miners can continuously solve Bitcoin's proof-of-work problem, it will undermine the decentralized nature of mining, turning this global industry into an oligopoly dominated by quantum technology holders. These risks will completely reshape the market landscape before the so-called "20-40 year security window" arrives. Post-quantum cryptography is inevitable, but it must be deployed before attackers acquire the necessary hardware, not as a post-hoc remedy. The NIST standard provides a roadmap, but not absolute guarantees. The transition will be lengthy, controversial, and disruptive. Attempting to postpone it for decades could expose Bitcoin and the entire crypto ecosystem to the most severe security challenges of this century. For the past 15 years, the crypto industry has been defending decentralization, trustlessness, and user sovereignty. Now, quantum computing presents a new challenge: will the industry act proactively, or wait for a crisis to erupt before reacting passively? The cost of misjudging far outweighs the cost of prior preparation. Many might believe Bitcoin still has decades of buffer time, but evidence points to a completely different conclusion: the clock of the quantum threat is already ticking, and the market is quietly adjusting. The only question is whether the industry can act before time runs out.

data.title: CZ's full Davos interview: Bullish on tokenization, payments, and artificial intelligence.
data.description:
Compiled by: CoolFish, Source: @kricsp

an

Moderator: Over the past few days at the forum, I felt that almost two very different dialogues were taking place here. One was a discussion about geopolitics and macroeconomics, concerns about the fragmentation of the global trading system, debt, and the role of the US dollar. These dialogues were often very frustrating and worrying. The other focus was on discussions about artificial intelligence, innovation, and technology. These discussions about future possibilities were vibrant, and many exciting technological breakthroughs are taking place.

In today's seminar, we will take a bold step by merging these diverse discussions—focusing not only on how innovation occurs and what its future holds, but also on the regulatory and geopolitical context in which these innovations occur, and how these contexts influence financial innovation. We have four distinguished guests. First, we have Steven van Rijswijk, CEO of ING Group from the Netherlands. Next, Jayee Koffey, Chief Enabler and Global Affairs Officer of BNY Mellon. Then, Fred Hu, Founder, Chairman, and CEO of Primavera Capital from China. Finally, Changpeng Zhao, founder of Binance from the UAE—we usually call him CZ, so I don't have to struggle with that name anymore. CZ is now free to speak without any restrictions, and I expect he will deliver a particularly insightful presentation. The theme of this discussion is how technology is reshaping the global financial landscape. Those who follow this field should be aware of the numerous changes currently taking place. Payment methods and monetary systems are undergoing dramatic transformations, and the underlying infrastructure for financial transactions is rapidly iterating. These changes are overwhelming. To begin the discussion, I will ask each of you in turn, seated in order: What do you see as the most profound restructuring? What development direction excites you the most? This could be a specific market sector—private lending, investment, Bitcoin, stablecoins, digital currencies, etc.; it could be a technological breakthrough—Bitcoin ledger technology; or it could be an innovation at the infrastructure level. The discussion is completely open; please speak freely. Please discuss which exciting new developments you believe will have the most profound impact on the financial sector. CZ: Absolutely. I focus on a very narrow area within the financial markets, primarily cryptocurrencies, blockchain, Web3, whatever you call it. I'm convinced this technology is a game-changer. And I think we've proven it won't disappear over the past 15 or 16 years. Binance is one of the world's largest cryptocurrency exchanges. Fred Hu: It really is the largest. CZ: It's the largest to date, larger than the top five exchanges combined. But look at some numbers—Binance has 300 million users. It's probably bigger than any bank I know. Its trading volume not only surpasses the Shanghai Stock Exchange, but last year it even surpassed the New York Stock Exchange. But currently, the truly mature industry in the crypto space consists of only two major sectors: exchanges and stablecoins, both of which are massive business systems. I'm very excited about three other new areas. I think tokenization is a huge area—I'm discussing asset tokenization solutions with more than a dozen governments, which can first realize financial benefits and then drive the upgrading of industries such as mining and trading markets. In the payments field, we've tried but haven't truly conquered it—to be precise, cryptocurrency hasn't truly entered the payments field yet. We've tried, but nobody actually uses cryptocurrency for payments. However, we're now seeing traditional payment methods quietly merging with crypto technology: when consumers swipe their cards, cryptocurrency is deducted from their accounts, while merchants receive settlements in fiat currencies like USD or EUR. Once these bridges are built, the payments field will see a major breakthrough. The third area, as you've all mentioned, is artificial intelligence. The native currency for AI agents will be cryptocurrency. Cryptocurrency blockchain will become the most native technical interface for AI agents. Current AI is far from being a smart agent; it can't buy you tickets or pay for your meals—but when it does, all payments will be completed through cryptocurrency. Host: That's the best-case scenario, the exciting part. Now I want to shift the topic and talk about how, whenever we go through a period of innovation and experimentation, some attempts succeed while others fail. So I want to explore what might not work. Ten years from now, if we were sitting on this panel in Davos, what developments being discussed today might not even be mentioned anymore? They might be obsolete. I'll offer a few ideas to get the ball rolling. You're all very excited about AI. But there's also some research from MIT showing that while AI can quickly accomplish a lot of work, its output is rather mediocre. They call it "work slop." You can get about 80% accuracy, and if you think that's fine, then AI is great. But if you really want to pursue excellence and ensure absolute accuracy, AI might have limitations. There's also the experiment with Bitcoin. El Salvador is pushing Bitcoin very hard. This is a good approach—El Salvador relies heavily on remittances and doesn't have its own stable currency. Bitcoin was supposed to be the ideal choice for remittances, reducing transaction costs, but despite significant marketing and resource investment, actual adoption is near zero. These are just a few examples of potential limitations for your consideration. Now let's continue. In the same order. What are some areas that people are excited about today, or at least some are enthusiastic about—but you think won't even be discussed in ten years? CZ, which areas should we be wary of right now? Or which areas are not worth investing in? CZ: Absolutely. I think all three of my guests are very tactful and politically correct. I'll give a more direct answer, which might offend more people, including those in my industry. I do agree with Stephen's point that if you asked me this question 10 years ago, I might have said Bitcoin payments. But ten years later, we're still far from that goal. So I remain skeptical of the payments sector. We are working on it. The entire industry is investing in numerous payment projects. But any innovative field comes with a very high failure rate, and the few success stories will grow exponentially, right? I also agree with Stephen's view on the metaverse. Okay, we've seen NFTs go through a really hot phase, but now they're pretty much dead. I have a strong feeling that memes might follow a similar path. I might be wrong. Many people in the crypto world will hate me for saying this. But, you know, there's a very high risk in new areas where value is highly speculative. Building use cases for these areas is difficult. Some memes have indeed survived, like Dogecoin, which has been around for about 15 years. So projects with cultural value may endure. But I think most memes won't last. And then I'd like to add, although this might offend other industries, I think physical banks will significantly decrease over the next 10 years. CZ: Yes, it will decrease. The need for people to go to physical banks will decrease significantly. I think ING pioneered online banking 25 years ago. It's clear that replacing traditional industries takes a long time. But today we have cryptocurrency and blockchain technology. Electronic KYC and e-everything already meet the needs of financial services, and the necessity of physical banks is decreasing. I don't think banks will disappear. They play a vital role—in fact, multiple vital roles. But all industries, whether emerging or traditional, carry risks. We should regularly and prudently assess market dynamics. Fred Hu: But you've only told us what failed in the last 10 years. You haven't told us what will fail in 10 years. CZ: Basically, you're saying that memes seem high-risk. Real banks are high-risk. I could go on, but that would offend even more people. Guest: Yeah. Host: No, I think your last point is very important. I wanted you to talk about banks. If you involve macroeconomists in this discussion, they'll be very concerned about the risks banks face. While banks remain a key source of financing for investment and growth—especially for SMEs, particularly in Europe, and less so in other parts of the world—the role of banks may weaken as new financial models emerge, and funding channels may shift to other mechanisms. Therefore, I'd especially like to hear your further elaboration on the risks. Another major risk that macroeconomists and financial experts focus on is the risk of financial markets. Everything is much faster now. Last week I attended a seminar on artificial intelligence and the risks of algorithmic trading in financial markets. I believe the pace of events has indeed accelerated dramatically. For me, the Silicon Valley Bank incident served as a wake-up call long before the many innovations discussed. In the Silicon Valley Bank incident, we witnessed a bank collapse faster than ever before—even compared to the collapse of Washington Mutual during the peak of the 2008 global financial crisis. Those two banks experienced a run of about two to three weeks before finally failing, while Silicon Valley Bank lost 80% to 90% of its deposits in just one or two days. That two- to three-week run only resulted in a loss of about 10% to 15% of deposits. Silicon Valley Bank lost 80% to 90% of its deposits in just one or two days. The pace of events is far faster than ever before. More importantly, this crisis wasn't caused by emerging technologies like artificial intelligence or Bitcoin, but simply by a new trend arising from people chatting online. They don't all have to go to coffee shops to exchange news and rumors. And because of new technologies, people can withdraw money from their online accounts without even having to queue. So in a sense, that's outdated technology compared to what you're talking about. However, this has fundamentally changed the speed at which bank runs occur. When you consider the impact on the broader financial markets, you see much more momentum trading. When everyone is trading using the same algorithms, with AI doing the work so that humans don't even have time to press a button, this will trigger more severe losses, more dramatic volatility, and a whole host of risks. How worried should we be about this? Is there any way to address it?

CZ: I'd like to add a few points. I think it can be broken down into several separate points. I think the first point is that, all else being equal, faster and cheaper is always better. This in itself doesn't create more risk. Existing risks are simply made more prominent by the increased speed. But if banks implement a fractional reserve system, when funds are insufficient, people withdrawing funds faster will only accelerate the exposure of problems. But slowing down doesn't solve the fundamental problem; it only leads to more consumers being unable to withdraw money when they need it, getting stuck, so they're trapped. This doesn't solve anything. **Therefore, all else being equal, technologies that reduce costs and improve efficiency are always superior.** Regarding the doubts surrounding Silicon Valley Bank, we sense a completely different atmosphere of rumors within the crypto industry. The bank may or may not be facing difficulties. But our impression is that this bank is very friendly to cryptocurrencies. It may have been shut down in 2023 by "Operation Choke Point 2.0." Let me give another example of Binance: In December 2023, after the FTX crash, after the Luna and UST crashes, and actually after the Silicon Valley Bank incident, Binance's platform saw a single-day maximum withdrawal of $7 billion equivalent in assets. The system operated without problems. In that week, the withdrawal amounts were several hundred million, then a billion, then a billion, then 7 billion, then another billion, then another billion. During that week, a total of $14 billion in assets were withdrawn from the platform, and the system remained stable throughout. I don't know of any bank in the banking system that could withstand such a scale of withdrawals. The so-called "bank run" essentially stems from the design flaws of the fractional-reserve banking system. When a fractional-reserve system is adopted, liquidity problems arise. Ultimately, this is a system design problem, not an artificial intelligence problem. That's my point of view. Of course, artificial intelligence does have the potential risk of synchronized actions. But I think this is just the tip of the iceberg, and attributing all problems to this would be too simplistic. Host: Okay, so I have some different ideas. So the key lies in settlement, risk management, and regulation, including reserve requirements. Some work needs to be done internally by companies, but government regulation and national infrastructure construction are equally indispensable. We are very fortunate to have such a diverse panel of experts – each of you operates globally, with your companies rooted in different countries: the Netherlands, the US, China, and the UAE. Could you all discuss the importance of regulatory frameworks? I'll start with Fred. In particular, different countries have adopted different approaches to managing these risks. Fred, could you specifically discuss some of the different approaches taken by China and the US? How do government practices affect opportunities in these countries? CZ: I have a different perspective on this issue because we are in different industries. Personally, I think the regulation of the banking and securities industries is highly developed, mature, and similar in various countries. Of course, there are differences, which might be a simplistic interpretation for a novice. But cryptocurrency regulation is quite different – ​​current policies vary greatly from country to country. Frankly, Binance has about 22 or 23 licenses around the world, but most countries in the world today do not have a licensing system. We've also seen rapid progress in the US, but it's still in progress, right? Regarding market structure, the Genius Act was passed last year, only six or seven months ago. So it's an ongoing process. We've also seen many other countries, such as the UAE, which have implemented quite forward-looking regulatory policies, as well as Bahrain, Pakistan, and Kenya. We're happy to participate in the consultation process with them so they can at least engage in dialogue with industry participants. I served as a private advisor to some governments—although I'm neither a cryptocurrency expert nor a regulatory expert. But I was simply speaking from the perspective of market participants. Furthermore, there are some key differences in national policies during this process, particularly regarding capital controls. Many countries restrict capital outflows, with any excess constituting money laundering or substantial illegality (however defined). The US doesn't have this problem. It doesn't have such controls. Tax systems vary greatly from country to country, which directly impacts financial regulation—for example, should the tax be levied on unrealized gains or realized gains when the price of Bitcoin rises after purchase? Clearly, clearer and more unified rules would greatly improve the situation. However, I think a global regulatory body is difficult to achieve, although not entirely impossible at present. Different countries have different priorities, different agendas, and different considerations, so a unified global regulatory body would be quite difficult. We would love to see that, especially if that global regulatory body could develop a positive and relatively innovation-oriented regulatory framework. That would make the work of industry players much easier. But frankly, logically it should be—after all, cryptocurrencies are essentially the same across countries. We don't want to change with each country, so there should be a best-practice framework that we can implement. I've actually spent a lot of time trying to figure out what that is and how to work with different countries. Host: It's good to hear that, I agree. Now may not be the time to launch a new global international organization or a new global regulatory framework. That will be a tough battle. But that doesn't mean we shouldn't start thinking about what it would look like if the opportunity actually came, especially if we encountered some kind of crisis or major financial collapse. We might want to have some ideas and plans in place so that some solutions can be implemented when the time comes. CZ: A relatively easy thing to implement is a regulatory passporting system. Like once you obtain a license in one country, it can be recognized in other countries. This only requires some kind of agreement between the regulatory agencies of different countries. We have already seen some related discussions. I think this step is most likely to be implemented first. Creating new regulatory agencies or even global organizations like forums is both difficult to implement and time-consuming.

data.title: Amazon Set to Cut 14,000 Corporate Jobs Next Week as Part of 30,000-Role Streamlining Plan
data.description:
Amazon Prepares For Record Job Cuts As AI Reshapes The Workplace

Amazon is tightening its belt once again.

The e-commerce and cloud giant is reportedly readying a second wave of corporate layoffs, scheduled to begin as early as 27 January 2026.

This move is part of a massive internal restructuring aimed at transforming the company into a leaner, more agile operation that puts artificial intelligence at the heart of everything it does.

While the company manages a global workforce of 1.58 million people, these specific cuts are aimed squarely at the white-collar heart of the business.

Amazon $AMZN is reportedly planning to cut thousands more corporate jobs next week - Reuters pic.twitter.com/VwCwPtFv1e

— Evan (@StockMKTNewz) January 22, 2026

Why Is Amazon Trimming Its Corporate Sails?

The primary driver behind this shift is a dual focus on eliminating corporate bloat and leaning into automation.

CEO Andy Jassy has been vocal about the need to strip away the layers of management that accumulated during years of rapid expansion.

During a call with analysts, Jassy explained that the reduction was "not really financially driven and it’s not even really AI-driven."

Instead, he pointed to the company’s internal environment, stating, "it’s culture," and noting that when a company grows as quickly as Amazon has, "you end up with a lot more people than what you had before, and you end up with a lot more layers."

Which Departments Will Feel The Impact Of The New Cuts?

While the majority of Amazon’s staff work in the sprawling network of fulfilment centres and warehouses, this round of layoffs targets the offices.

Around 14,000 roles are expected to be cut next week, matching the scale of the reductions seen in October 2025.

Sources suggest the axe will fall on several high-profile divisions, including Amazon Web Services (AWS), the retail segment, Prime Video, and the human resources department, known internally as People Experience and Technology.

This initiative brings the total goal to roughly 30,000 corporate roles eliminated, representing about 10% of Amazon's office-based workforce.

🚨 Amazon layoffs are back

🔹️30,000 corporate jobs targeted
🔹️Thousands more cuts next week
🔹️Hits AWS, Retail, Prime Video, HR
🔹️Nearly 10% of corporate workforce
🔹️CEO Andy Jassy says it’s “culture & bureaucracy” not AI or finances

Largest layoff in Amazon’s history pic.twitter.com/ollJaLby3P

— Shazi (@ShaziGoalie) January 23, 2026

Is Artificial Intelligence Taking Over Human Roles?

There is a slight tension in the company’s messaging regarding the role of technology.

In an internal letter regarding previous cuts, the company described this generation of AI as "the most transformative technology we’ve seen since the internet," claiming it allows for much faster innovation.

Jassy also remarked earlier in 2025 that he expected the corporate headcount to shrink over time due to "efficiencies gained from the use of AI."

By automating routine, repetitive tasks and using AI agents to write software code, Amazon is effectively redesigning job descriptions to require fewer people for the same output.

How Is The Company Supporting Departing Staff?

For those caught in the transition, Amazon has historically offered a window of stability.

Workers affected in the previous October round were kept on the payroll for 90 days, a period that is set to expire on 26 January 2026.

Amazon US announced on October 28, 2025, the layoff of about 14,000 corporate employees due to AI efficiency and over-hiring correction. Affected staff received early-morning text notifications and will get 90 days of pay and benefits.

— Takechiyo (@takechiyo0705) October 29, 2025

This time allowed employees to either seek new roles within the company’s high-growth areas, such as AI development and cloud infrastructure, or find external employment.

While severance packages and transition assistance are generally provided, the specific details often vary depending on the region and the specific job function of the employee.

The Real Cost Of Corporate Efficiency

Coinlive views this restructuring as a defining moment for the modern tech landscape.

Amazon is moving beyond simple cost-cutting; it is actively auditioning for a future where human oversight is a premium rather than a standard.

By stripping away 10% of its corporate mind-share to solve a culture problem of bureaucracy, the company is betting that software can manage what managers used to.

30,000 isn’t a ‘layoff number’...it’s a REAL signal. When a company like Amazon starts cutting at scale, it usually means demand is slowing + automation is accelerating + they’re protecting margins for Wall Street. Job security in 2026 is a fairy tale.

— The Grind Hotline (@thegrindhotline) January 22, 2026

This shift forces a difficult question upon the global market.

If a company can grow its bottom line while systematically shrinking its human footprint, does the traditional corporate career path even exist anymore?

Innovation is now being measured by how many people a company can afford to let go while remaining operational.

data.title: Twilight Zone: The Crypto Economy in 2026 and Beyond
data.description:
Author: Ryan Watkins, Co-founder of Syncracy Capital; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • In 2021, expectations for crypto assets were too high; valuations have gradually returned to rationality since then, and the valuations of high-quality assets are now approaching reasonable levels.

  • With the relaxation of the US regulatory environment, the pricing and value capture of tokens have finally turned around, making tokens more valuable for investment.

  • The growth of the crypto economy is shifting from cyclical to long-term, and some valuable use cases beyond Bitcoin have emerged in the industry.

  • Successful blockchains are becoming the standard for startups and large enterprises, and are the cradle of some of the world's fastest-growing companies.

  • Successful blockchains are becoming the standard for startups and large enterprises, and are the cradle of some of the world's fastest-growing companies.

  • After a four-year altcoin bear market, market sentiment has plummeted, and top projects are currently undervalued as multi-year investment opportunities, with few analysts predicting exponential growth. While top projects may thrive in the next phase of the crypto economy, rising delivery expectations and increasingly fierce competition from corporations will weed out weaker players. Nothing is more powerful than a well-timed idea, and the emergence of the crypto economy has never been more unstoppable. The crypto economy is undergoing the biggest transformation I've seen in my eight years in the industry. Institutional investors are accumulating cryptocurrencies, while pioneering cypherpunks are dispersing their wealth. Businesses are preparing for S-shaped growth, while disillusioned crypto natives are gradually becoming exhausted. Governments are leading the global financial transition to blockchain, while day traders are worried about the chart patterns. Emerging markets are celebrating financial diversification, while American cynics lament that it's all just a gamble. Recently, articles have proliferated about which historical period the current crypto economy most resembles. Optimists compare it to the post-dot-com bubble era, arguing that the speculative age is over and long-term winners like Google and Amazon will emerge, leading the industry along an S-curve. Pessimists compare it to emerging markets in the 2010s, believing that weak investor protection mechanisms and a lack of patient long-term capital could prevent asset prices from remaining high even as the industry flourishes. Both views have merit. After all, history, besides experience, is the best guide for investors. However, analogies are ultimately limited. We also need to understand the crypto economy from a macroeconomic and technological perspective. The market is not monolithic, but rather composed of numerous interconnected yet independent entities and stories. Here is my best assessment of where we are now and where we are headed. The Red Queen Cycle: "You see, here, you have to run at full speed to stay in the same place; to get somewhere else, you need to run at least twice as fast!" "—Lewis Carroll" In financial markets, many things depend on expectations. Exceeding expectations leads to price increases; falling short leads to price decreases. Over time, expectations swing like a pendulum, often negatively correlated with future returns. In 2021, the development of the crypto economy far exceeded most people's expectations. This was evident in some ways, such as the price-to-sales ratios of decentralized finance (DeFi) blue chips reaching 500x, or the valuations of eight smart contract platforms exceeding $100 billion. Not to mention the hype surrounding concepts like the metaverse and NFTs. But perhaps the most sobering chart is the Bitcoin/Gold ratio. Despite significant progress, Bitcoin's price against gold has failed to reach new highs since 2021, and has actually declined. Who would have thought that in Trump's so-called "crypto capital of the world," after the launch of the most successful ETF in history, Bitcoin, as digital gold, would perform worse than it did four years ago amid the systemic depreciation of the dollar? The situation is far worse in other areas. Most of these projects entered this cycle with numerous structural problems, making it even more difficult to cope with extreme expectations: The revenue of most projects is cyclical and dependent on rising asset prices. Regulatory uncertainty hinders institutional and corporate participation. Dual ownership structures lead to a misalignment of incentives between insiders and public token investors. Poor information disclosure results in information asymmetry between project teams and the community. The lack of a unified valuation framework leads to excessive market volatility and the absence of a basic price floor. These problems combined caused most token prices to plummet, with only a very few barely approaching their 2021 highs. This had a huge psychological impact, as nothing in life is more frustrating than continuous effort without reward. The disappointment is particularly acute for speculators and opportunists who saw cryptocurrency as a shortcut to wealth. Over time, this predicament has led to widespread burnout across the industry. This is certainly a healthy trend. The days of minimal investment yielding spectacular returns should be over, and the era of making fortunes from speculative products, as seen before 2022, is clearly unsustainable. However, thankfully, the aforementioned issues are widely understood, and prices reflect this. Today, few cryptocurrency investors, apart from Bitcoin, are willing to consider long-term fundamental analysis of any other asset. After four years of stagnation, cryptocurrency now possesses the necessary conditions for another upward breakout. alt="uXz8Aw5P2jmQkiIhSb89O5wqyo6q4YhF5P4IeMFS.jpeg">

    Crypto Economic Awakening

    As mentioned earlier, the crypto economy entered this cycle with many structural problems. The good news is that these problems are now widely recognized, and many of them are now a thing of the past.

    First, besides digital gold, many other use cases are experiencing compound growth, and even more are undergoing transformation. In the past few years, the crypto economy has spawned:

    • Peer-to-peer internet platforms enable users to transact and fulfill contractual relationships without the need for government or corporate intermediaries.

    • Digital dollars can be stored and transferred anywhere on Earth with internet access, providing a cheap and reliable currency for billions of people.

    • Licenseless exchanges allow anyone, anywhere, to trade top global assets 24/7 on a transparent platform, covering any asset class.

    • New derivatives such as event contracts and perpetual contracts can respectively provide society with valuable predictive information and a more efficient price discovery mechanism.

    • New derivatives such as event contracts and perpetual contracts can respectively provide society with valuable predictive information and a more efficient price discovery mechanism.

    • The global collateral market enables users to access credit without permission through a transparent, automated infrastructure, significantly reducing counterparty risk. A diversified issuance platform allows anyone, individual or institutional, to issue publicly traded assets at extremely low prices. An open financing platform enables people around the world to raise funds for their businesses and overcome local economic constraints. A more scalable and resilient infrastructure is built by crowdfunding and distributing operations across a network of physical infrastructure operators. This is not an exhaustive list of all valuable use cases in the industry to date. However, the point is that many of these use cases have demonstrated real value and continue to grow regardless of cryptocurrency price movements. Meanwhile, as regulatory pressure eases and founders realize the costs of misalignment between equity and tokens, the dual-stakes-token model is being corrected. Some existing projects are consolidating assets and yields into a single token, while others are explicitly separating on-chain yields for token holders and off-chain yields for equity holders. Furthermore, as third-party data providers mature, disclosure practices are improving, reducing information asymmetry and facilitating more effective analysis. There is a growing consensus on a simple and time-tested principle: 99.9% of assets need to generate cash flow, with only a handful of exceptions, such as stores of value like Bitcoin and Ethereum. These frameworks will only be strengthened as more fundamental investors enter this asset class, and their rationality will increase over time. In fact, given time, the concept of self-ownership of on-chain cash flow may be understood as a scale-based unlocking similar to self-owned digital value storage. After all, what other way in history has allowed you to hold digital bearer assets and automatically earn returns every time the program is used, regardless of where you are on Earth? Against this backdrop, leading blockchains are emerging as the monetary and financial foundation of the internet. Ethereum, Solana, and Hyperliquid are experiencing ever-increasing network effects, thanks to their burgeoning ecosystems of assets, applications, enterprises, and users. Their permissionless design and global distribution have made their applications among the fastest-growing globally, boasting unparalleled capital efficiency and revenue growth rates. In the long run, these platforms are likely to form the foundation of a potential market for financial mega-applications, and almost all leading fintech companies are vying for this market share. Therefore, it's not surprising that Wall Street and Silicon Valley giants are pushing blockchain projects with full force. New products are being released almost weekly, covering tokenization, stablecoins, and various technologies in between. It's worth noting that unlike the early stages of the crypto economy, these projects are not experimental but mature, production-ready products, and most are built on public blockchains rather than isolated private systems. This trend will only accelerate as the lagging effects of regulatory changes continue to permeate the system in the coming quarters. With the regulatory environment becoming increasingly clear, businesses and institutions can finally shift their focus from "Is this legal?" "How the shift to blockchain can expand revenue streams, reduce costs, and spawn new business models." Perhaps the most telling point is that few industry analysts predict exponential growth. To my knowledge, many of my peers, both sell-side and buy-side, are reluctant to consider annual growth rates exceeding 20% ​​for fear of appearing overly optimistic. After four painful years, valuations have been reset, and now it's important to ask ourselves: what if this really does grow exponentially? What if dreaming again truly pays off? "Twilight Zone" On a cool autumn day in 2018, before another tiring investment banking workday began, I stopped by an elderly professor to chat about all aspects of blockchain. As soon as I sat down, he told me about a conversation he had with a skeptical stock hedge fund manager who claimed that cryptocurrency was entering a "nuclear winter" and was merely a solution to a non-existent problem. After briefly explaining the unsustainable burden of sovereign debt and the collapse of institutional trust, he finally told me what he had said to the skeptic: "Ten years from now, the world will thank us for building this parallel system." Although it's been less than a decade, his prediction now seems remarkably prescient, as cryptocurrency increasingly resembles a ripe idea over time. In a similar spirit, and the central theme of this article, it argues that the world still underestimates the projects under construction here. And most importantly for all of us investors, the multi-year investment opportunities in leading projects are undervalued. This last point is crucial because while the evolution of cryptocurrency may be inevitable, your favorite coin could ultimately go to zero. The inevitable other side of cryptocurrency is that it's attracting increasingly fierce competition, and the pressure is unprecedented. As I mentioned earlier, as institutions and corporations flood the industry, they will likely weed out many weaker competitors. This doesn't mean they will dominate and monopolize the technology. But it does mean that ultimately only a few native players will emerge as winners, leading the world to reshape the cryptocurrency landscape. The point isn't to be cynical. In all emerging technology sectors, 90% of startups fail. There may be more public failures in the coming years, but that shouldn't make you overlook the more important aspects. Perhaps no technology resonates with the zeitgeist more than cryptocurrency. Declining trust in institutions in developed societies, unsustainable government spending in the G7 countries, the world's largest fiat currency issuer openly devaluing its currency, the stagnation of globalization and the fragmentation of the international order, and a growing desire for a fairer system than the old one are all inextricably linked to the rise of cryptocurrencies. With software (especially artificial intelligence) continuing to sweep the globe and younger generations inheriting wealth from the aging baby boomers, the timing of the crypto economy emerging from its bubble is perfect. While many analysts cite Gartner's hype cycle and Carlota as examples... Classic frameworks like Perez's "post-hype" phase describe this moment, suggesting the best returns are over and a more mundane, practical phase is about to begin. But the reality is far more interesting. The crypto economy isn't a single, synchronously maturing market; rather, it's evolved from a series of products and businesses along different development curves. More importantly, speculation doesn't disappear as a technology enters its growth phase; it fluctuates with market sentiment and the pace of innovation. Anyone telling you the age of speculation is over is either disillusioned or ignorant of history. Skepticism is reasonable, but don't be cynical. We are reimagining how money, finance, and most importantly, the governance of economic institutions. This should be both challenging and exciting. Your task now is to figure out how best to capitalize on this emerging reality, rather than endlessly tweeting that it's doomed to fail. Because within the fog of disillusionment and uncertainty lies a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity, one that lies before those willing to bet on the dawn of a new era rather than mourn the fall of the old.

data.title: Musk in conversation with BlackRock CEO: Future AI development should focus on space
data.description:
At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Elon Musk speaks with BlackRock CEO Lawrence Fink. The topics covered are artificial intelligence, robotics, energy, and space—similar to his previous discussions.

There's some new news: Oversighted fully autonomous driving is expected to be approved in China and Europe next month.

Fink: Good afternoon, everyone! It's a pleasure to see you all here. This year's Davos Week has been fantastic. I hope everyone here can feel the many in-depth conversations we've had. Of course, there have inevitably been disagreements, but there has been much more consensus. And just earlier today, a peace agreement was successfully reached, which perfectly reflects the mission of the World Economic Forum: to build a platform for dialogue, promote mutual understanding, and drive problem-solving. This is our core value. Today, I am very honored to have Mr. Elon Musk with me. He came all the way from California to meet with you all. Elon, thank you!

Elon: You're welcome. Actually, I heard about this peace summit and was wondering if it was some kind of "puzzle-solving" project—cutting a piece from Greenland, a piece from Venezuela, to create a peace plan? Fortunately, we really did achieve peace. Fink: All we sought was peace. Okay, let me introduce myself again. I've been CEO of BlackRock since its IPO, and I'm very proud of that. The company has generated a 21% compound annual return for shareholders. And since Elon took over Tesla and led it to its IPO, Tesla's compound annual return has reached 43%. I want to use this data to tell everyone here, especially our friends in Europe: This is why citizens of all countries should invest in growth assets and in their own country's high-quality companies. Imagine how much more lucrative those pension funds would be today if they had invested when Tesla went public. Tesla's performance is truly astonishing; globally, no other company of Tesla's size has achieved such a high compound annual growth rate. Elon, congratulations! This data speaks for itself. Elon: This is all thanks to Tesla's unparalleled team. Fink: Now, let's talk about something more in-depth: the future of technology. I'd like to discuss artificial intelligence, robotics, energy, and space with you, and at the core of all of these ultimately boil down to engineering rigor, large-scale production, and efficient execution. Globally, few people possess Elon's vision and perseverance, capable of facing the challenges of these fields head-on, not only proposing innovative ideas but also implementing them across multiple technological tracks. This is why I think it's necessary for us to have this dialogue in Davos. Currently, you are deeply involved in four major fields: artificial intelligence, robotics, space, and energy. From an engineering perspective, what do these fields have in common? Elon: Well, these fields all face extremely high technological challenges. But the ultimate goal of all my companies is the same: to maximize the future possibilities of human civilization, to give human civilization a bright future, and to promote the expansion of human consciousness beyond Earth into the universe. Take SpaceX, for example. Our core mission is to continuously break through rocket technology, ultimately achieving the continuation of human life and consciousness on the Moon, Mars, and even more distant galaxies. Moreover, I believe we should always regard life and the consciousness we know as fragile yet precious. Because, as far as we know, there is no other sign of life in the universe. People often ask me, "Are there aliens on Earth?" I joke, "I am one," or "I come from the future," but nobody believes me. Maybe I really am from the future, but unfortunately, they don't believe me. Fink: Haha, okay. Elon: But seriously, if aliens really are lurking among humans, I'd probably be the first to discover them. After all, SpaceX has launched over 9,000 satellites, yet we've never encountered an alien spacecraft in orbit that we needed to avoid. So my conclusion is: we must assume that life and consciousness are extremely rare in the universe, perhaps even unique. If this is indeed the case, then we must do everything in our power to ensure that this "light of consciousness" doesn't go out. I've always compared human consciousness to a faint candle flame in the vast darkness, which could be blown out by the wind at any moment. This is the significance of SpaceX's commitment to achieving "multi-planetary species": even if Earth faces natural disasters or man-made calamities in the future, human civilization can continue. Tesla's mission, initially focused on sustainable energy technologies, has now expanded to include a vision of "sustainable prosperity." Artificial intelligence and robotics are essential pathways to achieving prosperity for all humanity. People often discuss how to eliminate global poverty and how to ensure everyone enjoys a high quality of life. In my view, artificial intelligence and robotics are the only solutions. Of course, these two technological paths also come with risks, and we must remain highly vigilant. We certainly don't want reality to resemble the scenarios in James Cameron's *Terminator* movies; the thought alone is terrifying. His movies are indeed brilliant, but we obviously don't want to experience the plot of *Terminator* firsthand. However, if we can achieve widespread and low-cost artificial intelligence, coupled with ubiquitous robots, the global economy will experience an unprecedented explosive growth. Fink: Elon, do you think this growth will be universally beneficial, or will it only benefit a minority? How can we promote this growth towards universal benefit? Elon: It will certainly be universal. Simply put, the widespread adoption of humanoid robots will completely change the economic landscape; economic output will equal the average productivity of a single robot multiplied by the number of robots. I can boldly predict that in a future scenario of positive development, the prevalence of artificial intelligence and robots will reach saturation, sufficient to meet all human needs. At that time, people won't even be able to think of anything that would require robots to do. Because goods and services will become extremely abundant, and the number of robots will eventually exceed the number of humans. Fink: But in that era, how will human values ​​and goals be reflected? Elon: Nothing can be perfect. But we must recognize a reality: **extreme material abundance and mandatory labor demand are mutually exclusive.** If some jobs must be done, and only a few are capable of performing them, then abundance is out of the question, and such growth can only be growth for a minority. Fink: Exactly, growth limited to a small scale. Elon: Yes, that's exactly what I mean. But if there are billions of humanoid robots in the future—and I firmly believe that day will eventually come—then everyone on Earth will own their own robot, and everyone will yearn for one. After all, who wouldn't want a robot that can help them take care of their children and pets? Many of my friends are facing the challenge of supporting their elderly parents. Fink: Indeed, and the cost is very high. Elon: Yes, not only is it costly, but more importantly, the current labor force structure is simply unable to meet the needs of elderly care. The number of young people is insufficient to care for all the elderly. Fink: Exactly. Elon: Imagine how wonderful it would be if a robot could carefully care for and protect our parents and elders. I believe such robots will soon enter every household. So overall, I am full of confidence in the future. Humanity is moving towards an era of extreme material abundance, which is very exciting. And without a doubt, we are living in the most magnificent era in human history. Fink: In this new era, do you think it is possible for us to reverse aging? Or, will we be able to witness the birth of anti-aging technology? Elon: To be honest, I haven't devoted much energy to the field of anti-aging. But I believe it's definitely a problem that can be solved. And I believe that when we truly understand the root of aging, we'll find the answer is incredibly simple, not some profound or obscure mystery. I dare say this because almost all the cells in the human body age synchronously. I've never seen anyone whose left arm is severely aged while their right arm remains young. There must be a "synchronous clock" behind this, precisely regulating the aging rhythm of the 35 trillion cells in the human body. However, death isn't meaningless. The limited lifespan of humans has evolutionary reasons. If humans could truly live forever, society might stagnate, everything would stagnate, and it would lose its vitality. Even so, I firmly believe that humanity will eventually find ways to extend lifespan and even reverse aging. This possibility is very high. Fink: I can't wait. Let's talk about the future. The artificial intelligence models, autonomous machines, and rocket technology you just mentioned all rely on the exponential increase in computing power, a significant expansion of energy supply, and large-scale manufacturing. So, what are the bottlenecks to achieving these goals? Furthermore, given such massive investment, how can we ensure the widespread availability of technological benefits and prevent them from becoming the exclusive privilege of a few? Elon: I believe that the widespread availability of technological benefits is an inevitable trend. Because AI companies will instinctively pursue as many customers as possible, and the cost of AI is becoming increasingly lower, decreasing dramatically every year—in fact, it's changing significantly almost every month. Fink: That's right, open-source models are everywhere now. Elon: Yes, the development speed of open-source models is very fast. The technological gap between them and closed-source commercial models is only about one year. Therefore, AI companies will inevitably push their technology to every corner of the world to compete for customers. Fink: But the problem is that achieving this goal requires huge investments: computing power, chips, chip manufacturing plants, power supply, etc. In your opinion, what is the biggest bottleneck in these areas? After all, these are all very costly fields. Elon: You've hit the nail on the head. I believe that the biggest bottleneck for the large-scale application of artificial intelligence is, ultimately, power supply. Fink: That's right, it's energy. Elon: Exactly. We see that AI chip production capacity is growing exponentially, but the growth rate of global electricity supply is only 3% to 4% per year at most. Clearly, it won't be long, perhaps even by the end of this year, before we produce more chips than the existing power system can handle. Of course, China is an exception; China's electricity growth rate is astonishing. Right now, China is building nuclear power plants with a total installed capacity of 100 gigawatts. However, in reality, solar energy is the main force behind China's energy development. As far as I know, China's annual solar panel production capacity is as high as 1500 gigawatts, and the actual annual installed capacity exceeds 1000 gigawatts. Of course, solar energy has intermittent issues. To achieve stable power supply, it needs to be paired with energy storage batteries. Roughly calculated, 1000 gigawatts of photovoltaic installations can be converted into about 250 gigawatts of stable power output. This figure is quite considerable, equivalent to half of the average annual electricity consumption in the United States. Fink: That's right. Elon: The average annual electricity consumption in the United States is about 500 gigawatts. In other words, China, relying solely on solar energy, combined with energy storage systems, could provide half of the electricity demand of the United States. Solar energy is undoubtedly the most promising form of energy currently available. Moreover, whether on Earth or looking at the entire solar system, the Sun is the absolute energy core, contributing almost 100% of the energy within the solar system. This is crucial. The Sun's mass accounts for 99.8% of the total mass of the solar system, Jupiter accounts for about 0.1%, and all other celestial bodies combined account for only the remaining 0.1%. Even if you threw Jupiter into a thermonuclear reactor and burned it all up, the Sun's energy contribution would still be 100%, since Jupiter's mass only accounts for 0.1%. Even if you conjured up three more Jupiters out of thin air and threw them into the solar system, burning up all four Jupiters and all other celestial bodies, the Sun's dominant energy position would remain unchanged. Therefore, the future of energy ultimately lies in the future of solar energy. This is why SpaceX plans to launch solar-powered AI satellites in the coming years. Space contains inexhaustible energy resources and doesn't require any land resources on Earth. Space is limitless; we can build extremely large-scale energy facilities there. I believe the final scale could even reach hundreds of terawatts. Fink: We've discussed this topic before. Could you share with the audience how large a solar power plant area would be needed to meet all of America's electricity needs? Which regions should be prioritized for site selection? Also, I'd like to ask, since it's technically feasible, why haven't we implemented it on a large scale? Elon: Simply put, a 100-mile x 100-mile (approximately 160 km x 160 km) solar power plant would be sufficient to meet the electricity needs of the entire United States. Such an area isn't actually that large. You could easily find a remote corner in Utah, Nevada, or New Mexico to place such a plant. Of course, for security reasons, we won't concentrate all photovoltaic facilities in one place. But the key is that it only requires a small portion of the US land area to solve the nation's electricity problem. The same applies to Europe. You only need to build a solar power plant in sparsely populated areas like Spain or Sicily to meet the electricity needs of the entire Europe. Fink: So why do you think Europe and America haven't promoted solar power generation on a large scale like China? Elon: Actually, Europe and America are also promoting it, but the progress is relatively slow. The crux of the problem is that the tariffs imposed by the United States on solar panels are ridiculously high. This directly leads to the artificially inflated construction costs of solar power plants, since the vast majority of solar panels in the world are made in China. Fink: What conditions do Europe and America need to meet to make such large-scale construction commercially viable? Elon: I can share with you the plans of SpaceX and Tesla. Our two companies are working hard to achieve a solar panel production capacity of 100 gigawatts per year in the United States in the future. It will take about three years to achieve this goal. Fink: That's a huge number. Elon: Exactly. I also hope other companies will join in and work together to promote the development of the solar energy industry. Of course, we can't control US tariff policies. But for other countries, Chinese-made solar panels are extremely cost-effective, and vigorously developing solar power is definitely a wise choice. Fink: I know you'll be releasing some major news about robotics soon. You showed me those robots when I visited the Tesla factory. Elon: Yes. Fink: You mentioned there will be billions of robots in the future. Specifically in manufacturing, how long will it take for these robots to be deployed on a large scale? How long will it take for them to be truly practical, valuable, and ultimately achieve the "material abundance" you mentioned? Elon: The pace of technological advancement in humanoid robots will be very rapid. Currently, some Tesla Optimus robots are already performing simple tasks in factories. I expect that by the end of this year, these robots will be able to handle more complex jobs, although initially they will primarily be used in industrial settings. By the end of next year, we should be able to sell humanoid robots to the public. At that time, these robots will have extremely high reliability and safety, and their functions will be very comprehensive, basically meeting all kinds of people's needs. Fink: Actually, we have already seen a similar speed of technological iteration in Tesla's car products. Tesla's in-vehicle software is now updated almost every quarter, and each update improves the vehicle's intelligence level, like constantly upgrading the car, this "mobile robot." Elon: That's right. Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) software is updated even weekly. Recently, an insurance company stated that Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) function is safe enough to offer a 50% discount on insurance to owners who have activated the feature. Fink: Does the insurance company monitor the vehicle's driving data? Is this a prerequisite for the insurance discount? Elon: Yes. However, I believe that, technically speaking, autonomous driving technology is already a solved problem. Tesla has already launched Robotaxi services in a few cities, and it is expected that this service will have widespread coverage in the United States by the end of this year. In addition, we also expect to receive supervised full self-driving approval in the European market next month. Fink: Is it really that fast? Elon: Yes. The approval process in the Chinese market is expected to be similar to that in Europe. Fink: Let's talk about the space sector next. Historically, space exploration has been a capital-intensive industry, long dominated by governments. Clearly, SpaceX has completely changed that model. However, the scaling up of the space industry was relatively slow at first, but now I see you accelerating it. Could you tell us how automation and artificial intelligence technologies are changing the economic costs of space exploration, construction, and operation? Elon: Of course. One of the key technological breakthroughs SpaceX hopes to achieve this year is **full reusability of rockets**. To date, no company has achieved full reusability of rockets, which is precisely the core key to reducing the cost of space launches. Our Falcon 9 rocket has achieved partial recovery and reuse of boosters, with over 500 booster recoveries to date. Unfortunately, the Falcon 9's upper stage burns up upon re-entry into the atmosphere after completing its mission and cannot be recovered. This part of the cost is equivalent to the price of a small to medium-sized passenger plane. Starship, however, is completely different. As the largest launch vehicle ever built by humankind, Starship is designed to be fully reusable. Fink: Starship is the rocket you plan to use to land on Mars, right? Elon: Yes, it can be used not only for Mars landings but also for lunar missions and large-scale satellite launches. I hope to complete the technical verification of Starship's full reusability this year. This will be a landmark innovation because once full reusability is achieved, the cost of space launches will be reduced by 100 times. Fink: Exactly. Elon: The economic logic behind this is the same as the cost difference between reusable and disposable airplanes. If an airplane is scrapped after every flight, the ticket price would be outrageously high; but if the airplane can be reused, the cost of each flight would only be fuel. We believe that this technological breakthrough will eventually make the cost of space launches lower than the cost of air freight, easily dropping below $100 per pound of cargo. This will make launching large satellites into space extremely cheap. Moreover, generating electricity using solar energy in space will be 5 times more efficient than on Earth. This is because in space, solar panels can receive sunlight around the clock, unaffected by day-night cycles or seasonal changes. Fink: And the temperature in space is very low. Elon: Yes, there's no atmospheric interference, and no sunshine, rain, or snow. Furthermore, due to the absence of atmospheric attenuation of sunlight, the efficiency of solar power generation in space can be increased by approximately 30%. Overall, the same solar panel generates five times more electricity in space than on Earth. Fink: Is it possible to transmit the electricity generated in space back to Earth? Or is this electricity only usable for space-related needs, such as building AI data centers in space? Elon: Building a solar-powered AI data center in space is undoubtedly an excellent choice. As you said, the low temperature environment of space is a natural advantage. If the equipment is placed in a shaded area away from the sun, the temperature can drop to as low as 3 Kelvin (approximately -270 degrees Celsius).
